author: Sanju Jain
artistdescription: Sanju Jain is a contemporary Indian artist whose delicate yet profound watercolor paintings, created by natural pigments, explore the intricate relationship between nature and the feminine spirit. Rooted in symbolism and emotional depth, her works speak in whispers, offering quiet spaces of introspection, healing, and transformation. Drawing from traditional Indian aesthetics while embracing a modern sensibility, Jain’s visual philosophy “From Seed to Cosmos” reflects a journey of growth, resilience, and interconnectedness. Through soft palettes and layered transparency, she invites viewers to witness the subtle power of creation, womanhood, and the silent strength within.

projects.title: One Landscape Artist Residency : Sanju Jain

sections.value:
2025 Shillim Institute/One Landscape Artist Residency : Sanju Jain.

During her 21-day residency at Shillim Institute, artist Sanju Jain entered into a deeply intuitive dialogue with the forests, mountains, and living textures of the Western Ghats. Her journey unfolded through immersive treks, quiet observation, and a sensitive exploration of the natural environment, where every leaf, flower, bark, breeze, and shifting light became a source of reflection and artistic awakening. She experimented with natural pigments extracted from hibiscus, turmeric, pomegranate, leaves, and organic farm produce, transforming the landscape into a living palette. Encounters with the Sahyadri terrain, the rare Karvi plant, dried mushrooms, wild grasses, and the meditative stillness of Bedsa Caves enriched her visual and emotional vocabulary. Guided by the scents, sounds, and rhythms of the जंगल, Sanju gradually moved from memory-led mark-making into an embodied creative practice. The residency became not only an artistic exploration, but a profound inner encounter—an experience of meeting herself anew through nature.

Sanju Jain’s body of work created during the Shillim residency emerged as a poetic response to the ecology, spirit, and sensory richness of the Western Ghats. Beginning with a series of intimate small-format paintings rooted in memory, her practice gradually evolved into a more immersive and site-responsive visual language shaped by direct encounters with the land. Using pigments derived from hibiscus flowers, turmeric, pomegranate, leaves, earth, and organic vegetation, she developed a vibrant natural palette that echoed the hues of the surrounding forests and farms. Her works drew inspiration from the intertwined fruits of the Karvi plant, dried mushrooms discovered on mountain trails, wild grasses glowing in sunlight, the shifting blue of distant hills, and the sacred geometry of Bedsa Caves. These paintings and collages moved between observation and abstraction, carrying impressions of tree forms, birdsong, animal presence, light, shadow, and the emotional resonance of the landscape. A significant extension of her residency was her engagement with children through two art workshops—one on the Shillim campus and another at a nearby government school—reaching nearly 50 students. Here, Sanju transformed art-making into an act of discovery, showing children that colours can be found in the flowers, leaves, soil, and textures of their own surroundings. One particularly moving moment emerged when a village girl, while playing with these self-made colours, began to create what she felt and experienced from nature—revealing, through her intuitive expression, a quiet yet powerful colour of hope. This moment reflected the deeper essence of the workshops: that creativity can awaken confidence, belonging, and wonder. Through Sanju’s gentle guidance and celebration of nature and art together, an artistic approach and living curiosity began to shine in the eyes of the other children too, turning the workshops into spaces of imagination, emotional connection, and joyful ecological learning.
